_clinical_notes: CRITICAL: Anatomical snuffbox (hollow between thumb extensor tendons on dorsal-radial wrist) tenderness after FOOSH = scaphoid fracture until proven otherwise — high miss rate, significant morbidity if missed. Refer even if X-ray negative. Distal radius fracture: obvious swelling/deformity after FOOSH, especially in young athletes. TFCC (ulnar side, clicking): can manage conservatively if mild but should be evaluated. Grades: I = micro-tear, II = partial, III = complete ligament rupture.

mechanism.prompt: How did the wrist get injured?

deformity_wrist.prompt: Is there visible deformity, significant swelling, or a 'dinner fork' shape to the wrist?

options.label: Yes, obviously deformed or extremely swollen

snuffbox.prompt: Press into the small hollow on the thumb side of your wrist (the 'snuffbox' — just below the base of your thumb). Is it very tender?

location_wrist.prompt: Where is most of the pain?

click_wrist.prompt: Do you notice a clicking or clunking sensation when you move the wrist?

function_wrist.prompt: Can you make a fist and grip something?

options.label: Yes, full grip with some pain
Cannot grip or make a fist

outcome.seek_care_reason: Visible deformity, extreme swelling, or inability to grip after a wrist injury may indicate a fracture. You need an X-ray.

Tenderness in the anatomical snuffbox after a wrist injury strongly suggests a scaphoid fracture — one of the most commonly missed fractures. You need an X-ray (and possibly MRI) even if the X-ray looks normal.
Clicking on the ulnar (pinky) side of the wrist may indicate a TFCC (triangular fibrocartilage complex) tear. This benefits from evaluation to confirm and guide treatment.
